In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese yokai|ja|日本の妖怪一覧}} * {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}}, {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}} * {{ill|Hanuman|ja|ハヌマーン}} - A Hindu monkey god, believed by {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|ja|南方熊楠}} to be a source of Japan's Kōshin beliefs.

Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />

revision.text:
{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|ja|author=Kotanicho Teruhiko et al.|editor=Watanabe Shizuo|title=Japanese Folklore Encyclopedia|year=1999|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=978-4-642-01332-1}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Shinohara Tetsu|title=Encyclopedia of Japan|year=1986|publisher=Shogakukan|isbn=4-095-26010-6}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Nakamura Teiji|title=The Spiritual Power of Animals|year=1989|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=978-4-480-04131-9}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Murakami Kenji|title=Encyclopedia of Japanese Yokai|year=2005|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|isbn=978-4-04-883926-6}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese yokai|ja|日本の妖怪一覧}} * {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}}, {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}} * {{ill|Hanuman|ja|ハヌマーン}} - A Hindu monkey god, believed by {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|ja|南方熊楠}} to be a source of Japan's Kōshin beliefs.
{{DEFAULTSORT:Sarugami}} [[Category:Japanese folk beliefs]] [[Category:Mythological monkeys]] [[Category:Possession spirits]] [[Category:Sannō faith]] [[Category:Kōshin belief]]

The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

[[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Sarugami-qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Sarugami]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[zh:%E7%8C%BF%E7%A5%9E]] [[de:Sarugami]]

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.

[[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sarugami-qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Sarugami]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[zh:%E7%8C%BF%E7%A5%9E]] [[de:Sarugami]] [[ja:Sarugami]]

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sarugami-qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Sarugami]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sarugami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Sarugami]][[Category:11]] [[zh:%E7%8C%BF%E7%A5%9E]] [[de:Sarugami]] [[ja:Sarugami]]

{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神}}[[ja:猿神]] '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|sun deities|ja|太陽神}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神}}, as well as monkey {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|medieval|ja|中世}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社 (千代田区)}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|ja|蒼頡}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|ja|三上山}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|ja|野洲市}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>

Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three Wise Monkeys|ja|三猿}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|ja|今昔物語集}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|ja|宇治拾遺物語}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|yokai|ja|妖怪}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|ja|美作国}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}} [[category:Spirit possession]] After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|ja|ヤマタノオロチ}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|ja|光前寺}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen|ja|備前市}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito|ja|木頭村}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|ja|犬神}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|ja|author=Kotanicho Teruhiko et al.|editor=Watanabe Shizuo|title=Japanese Folklore Encyclopedia|year=1999|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|isbn=978-4-642-01332-1}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Shinohara Tetsu|title=Encyclopedia of Japan|year=1986|publisher=Shogakukan|isbn=4-095-26010-6}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Nakamura Teiji|title=The Spiritual Power of Animals|year=1989|publisher=Chikuma Shobō|isbn=978-4-480-04131-9}} * {{Cite book|ja|author=Murakami Kenji|title=Encyclopedia of Japanese Yokai|year=2005|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|isbn=978-4-04-883926-6}}
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese yokai|ja|日本の妖怪一覧}} * {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰}}, {{ill|Kōshin belief|ja|庚申信仰}} * {{ill|Hanuman|ja|ハヌマーン}} - A Hindu monkey god, believed by {{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|ja|南方熊楠}} to be a source of Japan's Kōshin beliefs.
