<!--Sarugami--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:猿神]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:猿神]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:猿神]] {{wikidata link|Q11572152}} [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Monkeys in popular culture]] [[Category:Mythological monkeys]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Spirit possession]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sarugami]][[ja:猿神]][[zh:猿神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sarugami]][[ja:猿神]][[zh:猿神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sarugami]][[ja:猿神]][[zh:猿神]]
{{ill|sarugami|ja|猿神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sarugami|qq=}} '''Saru-gami''' (猿神) refers to the monkey incarnations considered as messengers of {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|WD=Q511056|lt=sun deities|1=Solar deity}} such as {{ill|Hiyoshi-kami|ja|日吉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi-kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, as well as monkey {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=yokai|1=Yōkai}} appearing in Japanese {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval|1=Middle Ages}} folktales.
== Belief in Saru-gami == [[File:Hie Shrine Saru-gami.jpg|right|thumb|180px|{{ill|Hie Shrine|en|Hie Shrine|WD=Q702042|lt=Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine}} in Chiyoda, Tokyo, featuring Saru-gami]] {{See also|Japanese macaque#Macaques as mountain deities}}
According to ''Yōtenki'' (耀天記), a Shinto theoretical text attributed to a monk from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} on {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}, the legendary ancient Chinese figure {{ill|Cangjie|en|Cangjie|WD=Q1033199|lt=Cangjie|1=Cangjie}}, who is said to have invented Chinese characters, learned that before the appearance of gods, {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|1=The Buddha}} manifested as a deity at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} in Japan, taking the form of a monkey to signify omens. This led to the creation of the kanji "神" (god), meaning "to show through the monkey" (申に示す). It is also mentioned that Cangjie was a previous incarnation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and that soon after his enshrinement at Hiyoshi, monkeys gathered there. Although this story is fictional, it is considered an interesting narrative that connects Buddhist beliefs introduced to Japan with older native Shinto practices, explaining why monkeys were seen as messengers of Hiyoshi-kami.<ref name="Nakamura">{{Harvnb|Nakamura|1989|pp=45-61}}</ref>
In the ancient tale collection ''{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本現報善悪霊異記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Ryōiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本国現報善悪霊異記}}'', a story describes a monkey appearing to a monk at {{ill|Mount Mikami|en|Mount Mikami|WD=Q10865239|lt=Mount Mikami|1=Mount Mikami}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} (present-day {{ill|Yasu, Shiga|en|Yasu, Shiga|WD=Q611642|lt=Yasu, Shiga|1=Yasu, Shiga}}). The monkey claims it was once a king in India, reborn due to past sins as a monkey deity at a local shrine. Since Yasu is located across from Hiyoshi Taisha, it is believed that monkey worship spread around southern Lake Biwa from the 8th to 9th centuries, eventually influencing the view of monkeys as divine messengers in the medieval period.<ref name="Nakamura" />
Although Saru-gami has aspects of a sun deity, with "Hiyoshi" relating to the sun, the connection may also stem from monkeys' tendency to be active at sunrise. This association led to the belief that monkeys played a role in controlling the sun.<ref name="Nakamura" /><ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Tada Katsumi|title=Gensō Sekai no Jūnin-tachi IV|year=1990|publisher=Shinkigensha|isbn=978-4-915146-44-2|page=83}}</ref> However, as people moved away from agricultural lifestyles, the solar aspects of Saru-gami diminished.<ref name="Nakamura" />
During the spread of {{ill|Sannō Shinto|ja|山王信仰|1=Sannō Faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Shinto|qq=}} from the medieval to early modern periods, monkeys retained their role as divine messengers<ref name="Shinohara">{{Harvnb|Shinohara et al.|1986|p=712}}</ref> and were revered as mountain deities.<ref name="Kotanicho">{{Harvnb|Kotanicho et al.|1999|p=292}}</ref> The mediating role of Saru-gami between heaven and earth also linked it with local protective spirits, especially {{ill|Kōshin|en|Kōshin|WD=Q3082157|lt=Kōshin belief|1=Kōshin}}, {{ill|Sai no Kami|ja|塞の神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sai no Kami|1=Q115978710|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115978710}}, and {{ill|Jizō faith in Japan|ja|地蔵菩薩#日本における地蔵信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō faith in Japan|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q604687}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /> In Kōshin belief, the connection to monkeys ("申") inspired the widespread depiction of the {{ill|Three wise monkeys|en|Three wise monkeys|WD=Q835709|lt=Three Wise Monkeys|1=Three wise monkeys}} on {{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}.<ref name="Shinohara" /><ref name="Kotanicho" />
According to the ''{{ill|Ehon Taikōki|ja|絵本太閤記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ehon Taikōki|1=Ehon Taikōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11606863}}'', {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s mother dreamed of the sun entering her womb after praying to Hiyoshi-kami, leading to Hideyoshi's conception. The fact that Hideyoshi was nicknamed "Saru" (monkey) may be related to the Hiyoshi and Saru-gami beliefs.<ref name="Nakamura" />
== Saru-gami as a Yokai == [[File:Konjaku Monogatarishu Sarugami.jpg|right|thumb|200px|Saru-gami exorcism from the ''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'']] In medieval Japanese folktales such as ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' and ''{{ill|Uji Shūi Monogatari|en|Uji Shūi Monogatari|WD=Q2446713|lt=Uji Shūi Monogatari|1=Uji Shūi Monogatari}}'', Saru-gami appears as a malevolent {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=yokai|1=Yōkai}}. A well-known story from ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' describes a monkey demanding human sacrifices: {{Indent|In {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}), a giant monkey deity demanded an annual human female sacrifice. When a young girl was chosen, a visiting hunter volunteered to take her place. Hiding inside a chest with his trained dogs, the hunter ambushed the giant monkey and its followers, defeating them. The chief monkey spirit, possessing a shrine priest, pleaded for mercy, promising to end the sacrifices. The hunter spared it, and the ritual ceased thereafter.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Unknown|editor=Satake Akihiro et al.|title=Konjaku Monogatarishū|year=1996|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=978-4-00-240037-2|pages=28-33}}</ref>}}
After losing its divine status, Saru-gami was often mocked in folklore. A humorous example from the Muromachi period’s ''Fuji Bukuro no Sōshi'' illustrates this: {{Indent|In {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, an old man joked that he'd marry off his daughter to anyone who helped with farm work—even a monkey. A giant monkey appeared, helped with the work, and later abducted the daughter as promised. A nobleman rescued her, replacing her with a dog in a bag. The monkey returned, only to be killed by the dog.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Takashima Masae|title=Japanese Fairy Tales: Interesting Stories|year=1952|publisher=Chōbunkan|pages=59-63}}</ref>}}
Similar tales across Japan depict Saru-gami demanding sacrifices and being defeated by hunters or monks, often with the aid of dogs.<ref name="Murakami">{{Harvnb|Murakami|2005|p=157}}</ref> This motif echoes the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}} myth, believed to stem from ancient marriage rituals between deities and priestesses.<ref name="Nakamura" /> Notably, these stories often feature sacred dogs, such as {{ill|Hayatarō|ja|光前寺#早太郎説話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayatarō|1=Kōzen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388300}} from {{ill|Kōzen-ji|en|Kōzen-ji|WD=Q11388300|lt=Kōzen-ji|1=Kōzen-ji}} in Nagano, famed for defeating monsters like {{ill|hibagon|ja|狒々|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hibagon|1=Q4497985|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4497985}}.<ref name="Murakami" />
In the ''{{ill|Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja|太平百物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihei Hyakumonogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', a tale presents Saru-gami in a more eerie, ghostly context: {{Indent|In {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (now northern {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|WD=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}), a samurai staying in a haunted house encountered an old monkey that attacked him in an outhouse. After a fierce struggle, he killed the monkey, discovering numerous human bones in the house, victims of the beast.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Kōji Tokugawa Bun'en|title=Tokugawa Literary Anthology|year=1915|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|volume=4|page=367}}</ref>}}
These tales often portray monkeys as both comical and grotesque, reflecting the human tendency to mock creatures that resemble us yet seem less "evolved."<ref name="Nakamura" />
In {{ill|Bizen, Okayama|en|Bizen, Okayama|WD=Q863741|lt=Bizen|1=Bizen, Okayama}}, Okayama, and {{ill|Kito, Tokushima|en|Kito, Tokushima|WD=Q6418270|lt=Kito|1=Kito, Tokushima}}, Tokushima, Saru-gami is also considered a {{ill|possession spirit|ja|憑き物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/possession spirit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 憑依|1=Spirit possession|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1782082}}. Those possessed by it are said to become violently uncontrollable, with more severe effects than those of {{ill|Inugami|en|Inugami|WD=Q1150212|lt=Inugami|1=Inugami}}.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|author=Hino Iwao & Hino Yasuhiko|title=Animal Yokai Legends, Vol. 2|year=2006|publisher=Chūōkōron-shinsha|isbn=978-4-12-204792-1|page=268}}</ref>
